channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Cursor AI: 6 Apps Built with Cursor + Strategy Question for Builders
executive_summary: The video discusses how the AI code assist tool Cursor is being utilized to create various simple applications, such as a weather app and a chat bot. It highlights the limitations of Cursor for complex projects and emphasizes the need for design thinking in software development. The conversation also notes that software production is becoming more accessible, allowing even young children to build apps.
bullets: Cursor is being used for various simple applications, including a weather app and a chat bot. This shows how accessible AI tools are becoming for beginners. It's like having a bicycle that helps you ride smoothly without needing to learn how to balance first.

The context window of Cursor may limit its use for larger code bases, making it less effective for complex applications. Think of it like trying to read a long book with only a few pages visible at a time; you miss the bigger picture.

The rush to production and lack of design thinking contribute to the simplicity of the use cases built with Cursor. It's like cooking a gourmet meal without a recipe; you might end up with something edible, but it won't be a masterpiece.

Design thinking is crucial for developers to stand out in a world where software production is becoming easier. Imagine a crowded marketplace; to attract customers, you need a unique stall that catches the eye, not just a plain table with goods.

Cursor enables people to act on the advice of building simple Minimum Viable Products (MVPs). This means you can quickly test an idea without investing too much time or money, like trying out a new recipe on a small scale before hosting a dinner party.

Software production is increasingly accessible, with even an eight-year-old able to build a chat app. This democratization of technology is like giving everyone a paintbrush and canvas, allowing anyone to express their creativity.

Cursor plays a role in the larger Large Language Model (LLM) ecosystem by enabling non-coders to build functional and polished apps. It's like providing a toolkit to a group of artists who previously had no access to art supplies.

Examples of applications built with Cursor include a YouTube text search app and a Trello app copycat. These examples illustrate how simple ideas can be quickly turned into functional tools, similar to how a child can build a sandcastle with just a bucket and spade.

The video emphasizes the importance of design thinking in creating standout applications. Without it, developers risk blending into the background, much like a plain gray wall in a colorful art gallery.

The simplicity of the use cases discussed may reflect the current state of AI development and user experience design. It's akin to the early days of smartphones, where basic functions were prioritized over advanced features.

The conversation highlights the need for developers to focus on creating unique and engaging user experiences. If they don't, they risk losing users to more innovative competitors, just as a bland restaurant might struggle to attract diners in a bustling food scene.

The discussion around Cursor's limitations suggests that while it is a powerful tool, it may not yet be ready for complex software development. This is like using a basic calculator for advanced mathematics; it can handle simple tasks, but it won't suffice for more complicated equations.
key_takeaways: Focus on design thinking in your projects to create unique applications. This matters because it helps you stand out in a crowded market. If you skip this, your app may blend in with countless others, losing potential users.

Aim to build a Minimum Viable Product (MVP) quickly to test your ideas. This is important because it allows you to gather feedback without heavy investment. If you don't, you might waste time developing a product that no one wants.

Set a timeline for your projects, aiming to complete initial versions within a few months. This urgency can keep you motivated and help you adapt to user needs quickly. If you delay, you risk falling behind in a fast-paced tech landscape.

technical_terms.Cursor: An AI code assist tool that helps users build applications more easily. Think of it like a GPS that guides you through unfamiliar roads, making your journey smoother.
technical_terms.Minimum Viable Product (MVP): A basic version of a product that includes only the essential features needed to test an idea. It's like baking a simple cake to see if people like the flavor before adding frosting and decorations.
technical_terms.Large Language Model (LLM): A type of AI that can understand and generate human-like text. Think of it like a very smart parrot that can mimic human speech and respond in meaningful ways.
technical_terms.Design Thinking: A problem-solving approach that emphasizes understanding user needs and creating innovative solutions. It's like putting yourself in someone else's shoes to see the world from their perspective.
technical_terms.Context Window: The amount of information that an AI tool can process at one time. Imagine trying to read a book with only a few pages visible; you can't grasp the full story.
